questions.question: Explain the Win-Win approach to requirements negotiation and state its benefits.
keyPoints: Win-Win approach expands available options to satisfy core objectives of all parties
Benefits include: better stakeholder satisfaction, reduced conflict, improved commitment to deliverables
Creates collaborative environment rather than competitive
Results in sustainable agreements

questions.question: Describe the MoSCoW technique for requirements prioritization with an example for each category.
keyPoints: Must have: Critical features without which system fails (e.g., login authentication)
Should have: Important features that add significant value (e.g., password reset)
Could have: Desirable features that enhance experience (e.g., dark mode theme)
Won't have: Out of scope for current release (e.g., multi-language support)

questions.question: What is stakeholder analysis? Explain how you would create a stakeholder power/interest grid.
keyPoints: Stakeholder analysis identifies all parties affected by the project and their characteristics
Power/Interest grid: X-axis=Interest, Y-axis=Power
High Power/High Interest = Manage closely (key players)
High Power/Low Interest = Keep satisfied (meet their needs)
Low Power/High Interest = Keep informed
Low Power/Low Interest = Monitor

questions.question: List and briefly explain any four conflict resolution techniques used in requirements negotiation.
keyPoints: 1) Collaboration/Problem-solving: Work together to find mutual solution
2) Compromise: Each party gives up something to reach agreement
3) Consensus: Group discussion until everyone agrees
4) Forcing: One party imposes their solution (use sparingly)
5) Smoothing/Accommodation: Emphasize agreements, minimize differences
6) Withdrawing: Avoid conflict by postponing or withdrawing

questions.question: Explain the Kano model of requirements categorization with a suitable diagram description.
keyPoints: Basic/Must-be requirements: Expected features whose absence causes dissatisfaction
Performance/One-dimensional: More is better, linear relationship with satisfaction
Attractive/Delighters: Unexpected features that create delight when present
Diagram shows three curves: Basic as step function, Performance as diagonal, Delighters as exponential curve

questions.question: What is requirements triage? When and why is it used?
keyPoints: Requirements triage is rapid assessment and prioritization of requirements
Used when quick decisions are needed (tight deadlines, budget cuts, scope changes)
Based on: business value, dependencies, risks, implementation effort
Helps decide what gets included in release within constraints
Common in agile projects and change management scenarios

questions.question: Define the following terms: (a) BATNA (b) Negotiation Baseline (c) Scope Creep (d) Trade-off Matrix
keyPoints: BATNA: Best Alternative to Negotiated Agreement - fallback option if negotiation fails
Negotiation Baseline: Minimum acceptable outcome for each party - walk-away point
Scope Creep: Uncontrolled expansion of project scope beyond original boundaries
Trade-off Matrix: Tool comparing requirements against constraints (cost, time, scope) to visualize trade-offs

questions.question: Explain the requirements negotiation process in detail. Describe the various steps involved from initiation to agreement with stakeholder interests.
keyPoints: Step 1: Prepare - Identify stakeholders, analyze interests, set objectives and BATNA
Step 2: Explore - Discuss each requirement, understand priorities and constraints
Step 3: Negotiate - Use conflict resolution techniques, make trade-offs, find common ground
Step 4: Agree - Document consensus, create prioritized requirements list
Step 5: Validate - Review with stakeholders, ensure understanding
Key aspects: Communication, documentation, iterative approach, managing expectations

questions.question: You are leading a software project where the client demands 50 new features within 3 months, but your technical team estimates it will take 6 months. The budget is fixed. Using requirements negotiation concepts, explain how you would resolve this conflict.
keyPoints: 1) Conduct stakeholder analysis - identify all parties and their interests
2) Use MoSCoW to categorize requirements into Must/Should/Could/Won't
3) Apply requirements triage based on business value and dependencies
4) Create trade-off matrix showing cost, time, scope implications
5) Use Win-Win approach - explore options to expand possibilities
6) Negotiate realistic timeline or phased delivery
7) Document agreed scope with clear boundaries to prevent scope creep
8) Establish change control process for future modifications

questions.question: Describe the Kano model in detail. How does it help in understanding customer satisfaction? Give examples of each category from an e-commerce application.
keyPoints: Kano model categorizes requirements into three types based on customer satisfaction impact
Basic Requirements (Must-be): Features expected but not noticed when present (e.g., secure payment, order confirmation)
Performance Requirements (One-dimensional): Features where more is better (e.g., faster delivery options, more payment methods)
Attractive Requirements (Delighters): Unexpected features that surprise and delight (e.g., personalized product recommendations, free gift wrapping)
Model helps prioritize development based on satisfaction impact vs effort

questions.question: Discuss the various conflict resolution techniques in requirements negotiation. Under what circumstances would you use each technique?
keyPoints: Collaboration/Problem-solving: Use when all concerns are important, time available - achieves best outcomes
Compromise: Use when goals are equally important, need quick resolution - gives mutual concessions
Consensus: Use when group buy-in is critical, democratic approach - builds team commitment
Forcing/Imposing: Use in emergencies, when one clearly has better solution - quick but may cause resentment
Smoothing: Use when maintaining relationships is priority, temporary issues - defers problems
Withdrawing: Use when issue is trivial, emotions are high - avoids escalation
Each technique has appropriate context - skilled negotiator knows when to use each
